body:
### Problem

Related: <https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34204> <https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/32471> <https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/12242>

There are no clear guidelines on a how a plugin should restore its state when sessions are restored. I see two main scenarios:

1. Restoring buffers (e.g., fugitive)
2. Restoring internal data (e.g., nvim-dap's breakpoints)

While (2) could be labeled as a more "advanced" functionality, (1) has a direct impact on user experience. The default `sessionoptions` includes `nofile`, meaning that plugin buffers are restored _and empty_ (if the plugin is not handling the situation).

There are some approaches for handling (1), but no clear "recommendation". Some examples:

- Using `SessionLoadPost` to (just) wipe out the buffers. Minimal effort for implementation and at least gets rid of the empty buffers.
- Using `SessionLoadPost` to "fully" restore the buffers. The complexity highly depends on the use case; sometimes buffers might not be properly restored without recording some additional data (aka, (2)). Depending on the plugin's behavior, some other events may be used instead (e.g., `BufReadPost`, `BufAdd`).

For (2) there's also _at least_ a couple of alternatives:

- Leveraging the session file itself, with `'sessionoptions'` containing "globals". While this has the advantage of not having to touch another file, there quite a few drawbacks:
    - Only a subset of globals is saved: besides the naming convention (which is a bit odd IMO), only string and number variables are saved (related <https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/23010>). What this imposes is that plugins must encode their data, which is a bit awkward.
    - This relies on a non-default option, so only a minority of users actually get to see the "full restore" functionality
- Using a "sidecar" file, saved along the session file. This frees up any restrictions with what data gets saved, but introduces its own complexities, with questions like: where should the file be saved? More broadly, the plugin has the "responsibility" of fully managing the file's state.

For both approaches, hooking up an "autosave" is much easier now that we have `SessionWritePre` (<https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39688>), but, once again, there's no clear "recommendation" on which one should be used (and also, maybe there's something else I haven't considered).

### Expected behavior

I'm creating as an issue instead of a discussion, because I have a clear "completion" criteria: **a new help tag (maybe `lua-plugin-sessions`) that describes the "best practices" for dealing with sessions.** 

I'm well aware of the eventual Lua rewrite. But I think we should be "pragmatic" and have _some_ recommendation until that day comes (or, at least, _this issue could be closed_ with a big "there's no recommendation yet, we have to wait for the Lua rewrite!!"). The major caveat here is that I think it would be difficult to be "forwards compatible" with the rewrite: if the "vim way" was satisfying, it wouldn't need to be rewritten in Lua.

Personally, my take is:
- Using `SessionLoadPost` to restore buffers
- Using the "globals" approach, with `SessionWritePre`, to save additional data

I'd also lean towards making "globals" a default flag in `'sessionoptions'`, but that would deviate from vim, therefore making us evil or something.

comment_data.body:
> how a plugin should restore its state when sessions are restored

My first reaction is "they shouldn't" (and that could be the documented guidance), since in general it's a recipe for failure if a feature depends on plugins all doing the right little housekeeping things.

And we should also give guidance about *where* state should be stored so that it's correctly forgotten on session save/restored. Only in rare cases should plugins use uppercase globals which are preserved after session save/restore.

(Open to corrections)

labels.description: save/restore editor state: shada, context, ctx, sessions, mksession, undo, swap
labels.name: editor-state

title: guidelines for plugins to handle sessions (`lua-plugin-sessions` help tag)

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
